extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property G(opted_out implies not sent) requires that whenever opted_out is true, sent is false. The transition t_resume_glitch fires on send_attempt when (opted_out and ticks >= 8) and sets sent := true, directly producing a state with opted_out=true and sent=true.

Reachability: ticks starts at 0 and t_tick increments it up to a cap of 8, so eight tick events drive ticks from 0 to 8 (each also setting sent:=false, harmless here). Then optout sets opted_out:=true, sent:=false, ticks unchanged at 8. A subsequent send_attempt enables both t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) and t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out and ticks>=8). Since the spec gives no explicit priority resolving this conflict, a sound safety audit must treat the choice as nondeterministic; a safety property holds only if it holds under every resolution. Taking t_resume_glitch yields opted_out=true, sent=true, violating the property.

Trace: init (F,F,0) -> 8x tick -> (F,F,8) -> optout -> (T,F,8) -> send_attempt[t_resume_glitch] -> (T,T,8). The violating state is reached in 10 events, within the 12-event bound. Witness events: tick x8, optout, send_attempt.
